text: This is a video demonstration of robotic intersphincter resection in a lower third rectal cancer. The patient is a fifty-six-year-old gentleman with a body mass index of twenty-five kilograms per meter square and a good performance status who was diagnosed with moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the lower rectum. Colonoscopy showed a growth three centimeters from the anal urge and T-two weighted magnetic resonance imaging showed a lower rectal growth involving the internal sphincter with the inter-sphincteric space being free. A complete staging workup showed a T-N-N-M disease. Following neoadjuvant long course chemoradiation, he had a partial response and on digital rectal examination had a good sphincter tone. The adjacent pictures are his reassessment MRI showing the interspectric space being free on the coronal images.

The patient is positioned in a low lithotomy with a slight right lateral tilt for the robotic procedure using the da Vinci X-eye system. The surgeon targets the recto-sigmoid junction to avoid robotic arm collisions while operating in the retroperitoneum and pelvis. The medial peritoneal fold is incised to start medial to lateral dissection near the duodenal jejunal flexure. The inferior mesenteric vein is identified, developed, ligated, and divided at its root. Dissection continues between the white line of DALT and Gerota's fascia, extending to the inferior border of the pancreas. The medial peritoneal fold is scored from the sacral promontory, and the plane near the autonomic nerves is identified close to the inferior mesenteric artery. Nodal clearance is performed at the artery's root, which is clipped and divided at its origin. Lateral peritoneal dissection follows, mobilizing the left colon beyond the splenic flexure.

The surgeon initiates posterior pelvic dissection above the prehypogastric fascia, reaching the endopelvic fascia posteriorly. Anterior dissection is performed while safeguarding the seminal vesicles, entering the plane posterior to the Denonvilliers fascia. With the patient's left side elevated, the intersphincteric groove is identified and entered from the left lateral aspect, establishing the intersphincteric resection plane. This dissection plane is continued anteriorly and similarly performed on the right lateral aspect. The puborectalis muscle, forming a V-shaped sling, is identified and resected, completing the total mesorectal excision.

The surgical robot is undocked, and the perineal phase of dissection begins. A self-retaining Loonstar retractor is positioned, and a circumferential incision is marked on the anal mucosa with adequate margins. The dissection deepens into the intersphincteric space, identified by the glistening white muscle fibers of the external sphincter. The dissection continues circumferentially, and the specimen is delivered, followed by a proximal transection.
The surgeon performs an end-to-end colovenil anastomosis using interrupted absorbable sutures. A defunctioning ileostomy is created, marking the completion of the procedure.
